text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 14 November 2018

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures following head injury; persistent but improving memory and attention difficulties; hypertension; dyslipidaemia; ex-smoker; lives independently with community support. The epilepsy type is not specified.

Medication:
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily
- Carbamazepine (Tegretol Retard) 400 mg twice daily
- Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening
- Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Vitamin B compound, Thiamine, Calcium/vitamin D supplement, Emollients as required

I reviewed Bill in clinic today. He attended alone but brought a detailed seizure diary and phone reminder schedule that he set up with the community epilepsy nurse. He reports feeling more organised, with better sleep routine and regular meals. He is attending a local exercise class once weekly and walking to the shops most days. On examination he mobilised independently with a steady gait, rose from the chair without assistance, and demonstrated improved balance on tandem stance compared with his last visit. Speech was clear, orientation intact, and there has been a noticeable improvement in attention and recall with cueing.

Seizures: We discussed his recorded history in detail to clarify events. His first seizure occurred in May 2018 in Ireland, at night while asleep. He woke with rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a sense of déjà vu. The second event was in November 2018 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms. Since setting consistent phone reminders and maintaining a daily seizure and medication diary, he reports improved adherence and no further events recorded to date. There have been no witnessed episodes, injuries, or features suggestive of generalised convulsions. No emergency attendances.

Adherence and lifestyle: He uses timed alarms for morning and evening doses and cross-checks with a weekly blister pack; he has not missed doses in the last eight weeks per diary. Alcohol intake is nil; caffeine limited to one cup daily. Sleep hygiene has improved with a set bedtime and reduced screen use.

Investigations: Routine bloods six weeks ago were satisfactory (U&Es, LFTs, FBC within reference per GP copy). No dose-limiting adverse effects reported. Weight stable. No new focal neurological signs.

Plan:
- Continue current antiepileptic regimen unchanged in view of clinical improvement and absence of further events with good adherence.
- Continue seizure diary and phone reminders; bring logs to next review.
- Safety advice reinforced regarding sleep routine and medication timing; patient verbalised understanding.
- GP to repeat U&Es/LFTs and bone profile in three months or sooner if clinically indicated.
- Follow-up in neurology clinic in six months, earlier if further events occur.

Overall, his condition is improving, with better adherence through consistent seizure diary use and reminders, and no additional seizures since the two described events.

Yours sincerely,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: 1) Extract seizure events from the letter: two discrete seizures are described—first in May 2018 and second in November 2018; the letter also states no further events recorded to the clinic date (14 Nov 2018). 2) Count seizures: 2 total. 3) Define observation window: from the first recorded seizure (May 2018) to the clinic date (14 Nov 2018), which is approximately 6 months. 4) The patient is not seizure-free for ≥6 months because the last seizure was in November 2018 (the month of clinic). 5) Therefore, the current seizure frequency over the observed interval is 2 seizures per ~6 months → label as 2 per 6 month.
